text: The laparoscopic view shows standard port placement for a low anterior resection. A suprapubic port is used for introducing the endogias stapler for rectal transection in patients with a narrow pelvis. An additional port in the left iliac fossa aids in anterior retraction of the rectum and specimen retrieval. The uterus is elevated with a trans-facial suture for better anterior exposure. The rectal growth is visible on the anterior wall of the upper third peritonealized part of the rectum.

The surgeon holds the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) in traction and incises the medial peritoneal fold at the base of the rectosigmoid to enter the plane of loose areolar tissue behind the IMA. The dissection progresses into the holy plane of the total mesorectal excision (TME), revealing the hypogastric nerves covered by fibro-fatty tissue below the dissection plane. The ureter is visible and being pushed down during the medial to lateral dissection. The TME plane is developed for a short distance before transecting the IMA, with care taken to avoid injuring the hypogastric plexus by maintaining the fibro-fatty layer intact over the nerves.

The surgeon focuses on the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The left colic artery is visible, arising from the IMA and traveling cephalad. The IMA is identified as the major vessel arising anteriorly from the aortic surface. The fibrofatty tissue around the IMA is cleared, and lymph nodes at the IMA origin are taken with the specimen. The IMA is skeletonized, doubly clipped, and divided.

The surgeon uses a gauze piece placed anterior to the ureter as a guide to divide the sigmoid lateral peritoneal fold, freeing the sigmoid colon. The peritoneal incision is extended cephalically, mobilizing the descending colon and sigmoid up to the splenic flexure in a lateral to medial fashion. The ureter serves as a guide, and the white line of Toldt is visible, separating the colonic mesentery from the retroperitoneal fascia and Gerota's fascia. Blunt and sharp dissection along the white line of Toldt is used to mobilize the descending colon to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon completes the lateral to medial mobilization of the splenic flexure by separating the omentum from the distal transverse colon and dividing the gastrocolic and splenocolic ligaments. The linocolic ligament is divided, fully mobilizing the splenic flexure mesentery from the underlying gerota fascia. The rectal mobilization proceeds along the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, starting with posterior mobilization, followed by posterolateral dissection, and finally dividing the left lateral peritoneal fold. The hypogastric nerves are visible at the seven o'clock position, covered by loose areolar tissue, as the dissection remains close to the mesorectal fascia.

The surgeon joins the peritoneal incisions on either side in the cul-de-sac and proceeds with dissection into the rectovaginal space. The anterior mesorectum appears thin, while the posterior mesorectum is bulky. Dissection is carefully performed to keep the mesorectal fat on the anterior rectum and avoid injury to the posterior vaginal wall. The lateral ligaments, containing the middle rectal vessels, are divided using a harmonic device, staying close to the mesorectum to avoid nerve plexus injury.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the mesorectum five centimeters below the lower border of the upper rectal tumor, preparing the rectal wall for transaction with the EndoGIS stapler. The mesorectum is divided, avoiding injury to the rectal wall, and the site is cleared for stapler application. The sigmoid mesentery is divided approximately ten centimeters proximal to the tumor, ensuring the blood supply from the left colic artery and marginal artery remains intact.

The surgeon performs rectal transection using a blue or green stapler, depending on the rectal thickness. The rectum is pulled up in traction as the stapler is introduced through a suprapubic 12 mm port, ensuring a horizontal staple line. A second stapler firing aligns with the first to avoid dog ears. The rectal specimen is delivered through a left iliac fossa incision, and the segment containing the tumor is removed. An anvil of a 28 mm circular stapler is placed, and after anal dilatation, the stapler is introduced into the rectal stump for an end-to-side anastomosis. The completed anastomosis shows no twist or tension in the colon. Methylene blue is used to test anastomotic integrity, and a pelvic drain is placed.
